theme: Analytics tools, SQL verification, and cohort retention
objective: Verify that a plain-English data request and a SQL query match, identify the discrepancy between them, extract 3 specific insights from a cohort retention table, and produce a Mixpanel funnel analysis with 2 experiment recommendations.

realWorldAnchor.example: Razorpay's product team reportedly uses funnel analytics to identify exactly where merchants drop off during API integration — a PM reading the Mixpanel or Amplitude funnel can see whether the friction is in documentation, in the first API call, or in the test-to-live transition, and each diagnosis points to a completely different fix.

teamContext: Analytics tool fluency is the PM's interface with the Data team on a daily basis — a PM who can read a Mixpanel funnel, identify a cohort retention anomaly, and verify that a SQL query matches their data request compresses the time from 'I have a question' to 'I have an answer' from days to minutes.

tasks.title: Product Analytics Tools: What PMs Need to Know — Product School
tasks.summary: This article surveys the analytics tools a PM encounters in a modern product team — Mixpanel, Amplitude, Google Analytics, Looker, and custom dashboards — and explains what each is used for, what its strengths and limitations are, and what questions it can and cannot answer. For Pranav, this is the orientation layer before reading actual tool outputs in today's other tasks.
keyTakeaways: Mixpanel and Amplitude are event-based analytics tools — they track specific user actions (taps, clicks, form submissions) rather than page views, making them better for product funnels than Google Analytics, which is optimised for content and marketing attribution

A PM's primary use of these tools is funnel analysis (where do users drop off?), retention analysis (do users come back after their first session?), and cohort comparison (do users acquired in January behave differently from users acquired in March?) — these three question types account for most of the analytical questions a PM asks weekly

The biggest PM mistake with analytics tools is over-relying on the tool's default dashboard — default dashboards show vanity metrics (total users, total sessions) not product health metrics (activation rate, week-1 retention, funnel completion rate). A PM who has not configured custom dashboards is flying blind

tasks.title: Cohort Analysis: The Most Useful Framework You're Probably Ignoring — Lenny's Newsletter
tasks.summary: Lenny Rachitsky explains cohort analysis — grouping users by when they joined and tracking how each group behaves over time — as the single most underused PM analytical framework. The article shows how cohort retention tables reveal whether product changes improved retention for new users, whether certain acquisition channels produce better-retaining users, and where in the retention curve the biggest drop-off occurs.
keyTakeaways: A retention curve has three characteristic regions: a sharp drop in the first week (activation friction), a gradual decline through weeks 2-8 (habituation), and a flat tail (the retained core). Improvements in each region require completely different interventions — onboarding changes improve the early drop, engagement features improve the middle, and core value delivery improvements flatten the tail

Comparing cohorts over time is how a PM measures whether product changes are working: if the March cohort shows better week-4 retention than the January cohort, and a significant product change shipped in February, that is evidence (not proof) that the change helped

For Indian consumer apps, cohort retention is heavily affected by India-specific cycles: monthly salary credit dates, festive season spikes, and cricket match schedules all create periodic peaks that can make a cohort look artificially strong or weak if you do not account for the calendar

tasks.keyword: Razorpay merchant analytics product insights funnel drop-off India PM analytics tools 2022 2023
tasks.whyItMatters: Razorpay's product team works with a user base of merchants (businesses integrating payment APIs) rather than end consumers — their analytics questions are about merchant activation, integration completion, and recurring payment volume, not about individual purchase funnels. Searching for how Razorpay's PMs think about analytics gives Pranav exposure to B2B product analytics, which is structurally different from B2C and important to understand as a contrast.
whatToLearn: How B2B product analytics (Razorpay's merchant funnel) differs from B2C consumer analytics (Zepto's grocery funnel) — the key difference is that B2B users are slower to activate, have longer retention tails, and lose much higher value per churned user

What the key funnel metrics look like for a payments API product — API key generation, first test transaction, first live transaction, and recurring monthly volume — and how a PM diagnoses a drop at each stage

tasks.promptTitle: SQL parallel — verify whether a plain-English request and a SQL query match
tasks.claudePrompt:
You are a senior data analyst helping me practice the PM skill of verifying whether a plain-English data request and a SQL query actually match — a daily task when working with analysts.

Today I studied:
- Analytics tools: Mixpanel, Amplitude, custom SQL dashboards — what each is used for
- Cohort analysis: grouping users by join date and tracking behaviour over time
- How to read a retention curve and identify where the biggest intervention opportunity is

Real world context: I am an APM at Zepto. I asked my analyst for a specific data pull. She sent back a SQL query. My job is to verify it matches what I asked for before she runs it on production data — because running the wrong query wastes her time and delays my analysis.

MY PLAIN-ENGLISH REQUEST:
'Give me the number of new users who placed their first order in each of the last 6 months, broken down by the city they ordered from. Only include users who placed the order on Android. Sort by month, then by city alphabetically.'

THE SQL QUERY THE ANALYST SENT:
```sql
SELECT
  DATE_FORMAT(first_order_date, '%Y-%m')  AS cohort_month,
  delivery_city                           AS city,
  COUNT(DISTINCT user_id)                 AS new_buyers
FROM orders
WHERE platform = 'android'
  AND first_order_date >= DATE_SUB(CURDATE(), INTERVAL 6 MONTH)
GROUP BY DATE_FORMAT(first_order_date, '%Y-%m'), delivery_city
ORDER BY cohort_month ASC, city ASC;
```

Your role today: Act as the analyst. Answer my verification questions honestly. If I say the query matches when it does not, correct me. If I identify a discrepancy that does not exist, clarify.

Activity — 3 steps:
Step 1: I compare the plain-English request to the SQL line by line and state whether they match. I specifically identify any part of the SQL that does NOT match what I asked for, or any part of my request that is NOT reflected in the SQL.
Step 2: I write the corrected SQL line (just the one line that needs to change) to fix the discrepancy.
Step 3: I identify one thing the SQL assumes that I did not specify in my plain-English request — something the analyst had to decide herself when writing the query.

After all 3 steps, give feedback in EXACTLY this format:
WHAT WORKED: [bullets]
WHAT MISSED: [bullets]
INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE: [one insight about why city-level new buyer cohorts matter specifically for Zepto's expansion strategy — a national cohort table hides whether Mumbai retention is strong and Nagpur retention is weak, which is exactly the signal a PM needs when deciding whether to expand dark stores to a new city]
COACHING NOTE: [one specific tip for Pranav on verifying SQL queries as a PM — what to check first, what to trust the analyst on]

Begin with: 'Good. Go through the SQL line by line. Start with the SELECT clause — does it return exactly the columns I asked for? Then move to the WHERE clause. Tell me if everything matches, and call out any discrepancy you find.'

tasks.promptTitle: Excel/Sheets parallel — read a cohort retention table and extract insights
tasks.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM helping me practice reading a cohort retention table — a core PM skill for understanding whether product changes are improving user retention over time.

Today I also studied:
- Cohort retention curves: early drop (activation), gradual decline (habituation), flat tail (retained core)
- How cohort comparisons reveal the impact of product changes over time
- India-specific retention patterns: salary cycles, festive seasons, cricket match schedules distort weekly retention

Real world context: I am an APM at CRED. My analyst shared this 6-month cohort retention table showing the percentage of each monthly cohort that is still active (made at least one bill payment) in each subsequent month.

THE COHORT TABLE:

| Cohort | M0 | M1 | M2 | M3 | M4 | M5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Jan 2024 | 100% | 61% | 44% | 38% | 35% | 33% |
| Feb 2024 | 100% | 58% | 40% | 34% | 31% | — |
| Mar 2024 | 100% | 63% | 47% | 41% | — | — |
| Apr 2024 | 100% | 57% | 39% | — | — | — |
| May 2024 | 100% | 52% | — | — | — | — |
| Jun 2024 | 100% | — | — | — | — | — |

Note: M0 = month of acquisition. M1 = one month later. '—' = data not yet available.

Your role today: Act as the analyst presenting this table. When I extract an insight, ask me: 'So what?' — push me to connect every data observation to a product action, not just a data description. Do not let me say 'March cohort has good retention' without asking 'compared to what, and what product decision does that support?'

Activity — 3 steps:
Step 1: I identify which cohort has the best retention and explain why I chose that cohort as 'best' (what metric or comparison did I use?).
Step 2: I identify where the biggest retention drop happens across all cohorts and state what product intervention this points to.
Step 3: I identify one cohort that looks weaker than the others and form a hypothesis for why — naming one specific product or external cause.

After all 3 steps, give feedback in EXACTLY this format:
WHAT WORKED: [bullets]
WHAT MISSED: [bullets]
INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE: [one insight — May 2024 cohort's notably lower M1 retention (52% vs 57-63% for other cohorts) may be partially explained by summer seasonality in India: May is exam season and early summer, a period of reduced discretionary spending when users acquired via promotion-heavy campaigns may be lower-intent than usual]
COACHING NOTE: [one specific tip for reading cohort tables quickly — what the eye should go to first]

Begin with: 'Good. You have the table in front of you. Step 1: which cohort has the best retention? Before you answer, tell me: best on which dimension? M1 retention? Long-term M4-M5 retention? Or overall curve shape? Define your criterion first, then pick the cohort.'

bonusTasks.title: Amplitude vs Mixpanel — PM perspective on Indian startups
bonusTasks.keyword: Mixpanel vs Amplitude product analytics comparison Indian startup PM team 2023 2024 which tool
bonusTasks.summary: Most Indian product teams use either Mixpanel or Amplitude as their primary event analytics tool — searching for how PMs at Indian startups compare and choose between them gives Pranav practical context for the tools he will encounter when he joins a product team. Look for engineering or PM blog posts from Indian companies, not generic tool comparison listicles.

assessmentTask.taskDescription: Read a Mixpanel-style funnel for a CRED user journey, extract 3 specific insights (each tied to a data observation), and recommend 2 experiments — each with a hypothesis, the funnel data that motivates it, and a success metric.
assessmentTask.rawMaterial:
You are an APM at CRED. Your analyst shares the following Mixpanel funnel for the CRED bill payment user journey. This funnel tracks users from app open to completed bill payment over a 30-day window.

FUNNEL DATA (% of users who reach each step, out of all users who opened the app this month):

Step 1 — App Open → Bill Payment screen: 72% reach this step
Step 2 — Bill Payment screen → Credit card selected: 61% reach this step
Step 3 — Credit card selected → Amount entered: 54% reach this step
Step 4 — Amount entered → Payment confirmation screen: 41% reach this step
Step 5 — Payment confirmation screen → Payment completed: 38% reach this step

Additional context:
- Total users who opened the app this month: 1.2 million
- This funnel is for the primary bill payment flow (not CRED Pay, not CRED Store)
- The drop from Step 3 to Step 4 (amount entered to payment confirmation) increased from 10% last month to 13% this month
- Engineering did not ship any changes to the payment flow last month

Your deliverables:

INSIGHTS: Extract exactly 3 specific insights from this funnel. Each insight must: (a) reference specific numbers from the data, (b) say what the number means in plain English, and (c) state what product question it raises.

EXPERIMENTS: Recommend exactly 2 experiments. For each experiment: state the hypothesis (in the format: 'By [change] we expect [metric] to [direction] by at least [magnitude] because [mechanism]'), state which funnel drop motivated this experiment, and state the primary success metric.
assessmentTask.outputFormat: INSIGHTS: A numbered list of 3 items, each with (a) data observation, (b) plain-English meaning, (c) product question it raises. EXPERIMENTS: A numbered list of 2 items, each with hypothesis / funnel motivation / success metric.
assessmentTask.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM reviewing my work on Day 11: Analytics Tools, SQL Verification, and Cohort Retention.

I studied today:
- Product analytics tools: Mixpanel, Amplitude — event-based, used for funnel and retention analysis
- Cohort analysis: comparing user groups by acquisition date to measure retention curve shape
- SQL verification: checking that a plain-English data request and a SQL query match before the query runs
- India-specific cohort patterns: salary cycles, seasonality, and festive seasons distort retention curves

Real world context I should apply:
CRED bill payment funnel — 1.2 million monthly active users. Drop from Step 3 to Step 4 increased from 10% to 13% last month with no engineering changes. CRED's core product loop depends on completing this funnel.

MY TASK TODAY WAS: Extract 3 specific funnel insights and recommend 2 experiments with hypotheses and success metrics.

Here is my work:
[Pranav pastes his 3 insights and 2 experiment recommendations here]

Review my work and give feedback in EXACTLY this format — do not change these headers:

WHAT I GOT RIGHT:
- [specific bullets with explanation]

WHAT NEEDS CORRECTION:
- [specific bullets with explanation of why — especially check: are the insights data-specific or generic? Do the experiment hypotheses name a specific mechanism? Are the success metrics measurable or vague?]

BLIND SPOTS:
- [things I did not consider at all — e.g., the Step 3 to Step 4 drop increase (10% to 13%) with no engineering changes is the most interesting signal in the data and often goes unaddressed; the absolute number of users lost at each step matters as much as the percentage; or the possibility that the Step 2 drop (credit card selection) reflects users who have no credit card linked, which is a very different problem from friction]

INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE:
- [one insight about CRED's specific funnel context — CRED users pay their credit card bills once a month on or before the due date, which means the entire funnel compresses into a 3-5 day window around each user's bill due date. A funnel measured over a full 30-day window will look worse than a funnel measured in the 5-day window before due dates — this is a measurement design issue specific to bill payment products]

OPEN POINTS FOR TOMORROW:
- [1-3 specific, actionable items only]

SCORE: [X/10]
REFORGE COMPETENCY: Fluency with Data — [Needs Focus / On Track / Outperform]
